ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has an entry for Chinese broccoli as a raw ingredient (FOODON_03311737). No exact FoodOn entry exists for the prepared dish 'Braised Kailan with Oyster Sauce'; 'closest_parent' match used as it represents the primary ingredient.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chinese broccoli (Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra) — cooked vegetable dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers Chinese broccoli as a vegetable; 'closest_parent' match used to represent the cooked dish preparation with oyster sauce dressing, as a more specific code for 'braised kailan with oyster sauce' is not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared vegetable dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of braised kailan is approximately 250 g, representing a typical side-dish serving at a Singaporean Chinese restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Nutrient Composition of Food (HPB Foodo / Singapore Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on HPB Singapore food composition data for cooked kailan with oyster sauce dressing. The input record carried HPB-aligned values at confidence 0.7; values have been retained and cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked Chinese broccoli (SR Legacy) to confirm plausibility. Sodium at 340 mg/100g reflects the oyster sauce and soy sauce dressing typical of this preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, and B-vitamins are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid and micronutrient breakdown for this specific prepared dish is not separately reported in the HPB database.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008; University of Sydney GI Database — cruciferous vegetables analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised kailan with oyster sauce. GI estimated at approximately 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked non-starchy cruciferous vegetables (broccoli, cabbage family), which consistently fall in the 10–20 range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (15) × carbohydrate per serving (4.0 g/100g × 250 g serving = 10 g) / 100 = 1.5. This is a very low glycemic load.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 340 mg per 100 g from oyster sauce and soy sauce dressing
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced oyster sauce and soy sauce to lower sodium intake; a 250 g serving contributes approximately 850 mg sodium, which represents a significant portion of the 2000 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore HPB, and should be balanced against other low-sodium meals in the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (4.0 g/100g) and low GI (~15) with dietary fibre 1.6 g/100g supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Braised kailan is an excellent low-carbohydrate, low-GI side dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; a 250 g serving provides only 10 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 1.5, and the dietary fibre further attenuates postprandial glucose response — patients should request reduced oyster sauce to minimise added sugar from the sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.8 g/100g), zero trans fat, and kailan's sulforaphane and vitamin C content supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Braised kailan is a heart-healthy vegetable dish with very low saturated fat and no trans fat; the cruciferous vegetable base provides sulforaphane and vitamin C (16 mg/100g) with anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties beneficial for cardiovascular disease management, though sodium from the oyster sauce dressing should be minimised by requesting reduced sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory cruciferous vegetable with vitamin C and calcium; sodium from sauces may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Kailan provides sulforaphane, vitamin C (16 mg/100g), and calcium (40 mg/100g) which are anti-inflammatory and bone-protective — particularly valuable for RA patients on corticosteroids — but sodium at 340 mg/100g from soy sauce and oyster sauce may exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling, so reduced sauce is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Braised Kailan with Oyster Sauce (白灼芥兰, Bái Zhuó Jiè Lán) is a classic Chinese-Singaporean vegetable side dish made from kailan (Chinese broccoli, Brassica oleracea var. alboglabra), a dark leafy green with thick, crisp stems and broad leaves. The dish is prepared by blanching or lightly braising the kailan in boiling water until just tender-crisp, then dressing it with a savoury sauce typically composed of oyster sauce, light soy sauce, sesame oil, and sometimes a small amount of sugar and garlic. The result is a vibrant green vegetable dish with a mild bitterness from the kailan balanced by the umami richness of the oyster sauce. Texturally, the stems retain a satisfying crunch while the leaves are silky and tender. Nutritionally, braised kailan is low in calories (72 kcal/100g), very low in carbohydrates (4.0 g/100g), and provides moderate protein (2.4 g/100g), dietary fibre (1.6 g/100g), vitamin C (16 mg/100g), and calcium (40 mg/100g). The fat content (4.8 g/100g) comes primarily from the sesame oil in the dressing. Sodium is elevated at 340 mg/100g due to the oyster and soy sauce dressing. Clinically, this dish is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low glycemic index (~15) and low carbohydrate load. It is also suitable for cardiovascular health given its negligible saturated fat and anti-inflammatory phytonutrients. Sodium content warrants attention for hypertension patients. The dish is a staple at Singaporean Chinese restaurants, zi char stalls, and hawker centres, commonly ordered as a vegetable side alongside rice or noodle dishes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kailan with oyster sauce?
Is Chinese broccoli with oyster sauce low carb?
What is gai lan oyster sauce?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore food composition data for cooked kailan with oyster sauce, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central for cooked Chinese broccoli. The input record carried a confidence of 0.7 from an AI-generated GrabFood source; values have been validated against approved sources and are plausible for this preparation. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single HPB record for this exact dish preparation (blanched kailan with oyster sauce dressing) was directly matched with a unique HPB food code; the values represent an adapted match.
notes: 1. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for this vegetable dish preparation with oyster sauce dressing; no hydrogenated fats are used in standard preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, and mineral breakdown for this specific prepared dish (blanched kailan with oyster sauce dressing) are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (~15) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for cooked non-starchy cruciferous vegetables; no direct GI measurement for braised kailan with oyster sauce was found in the approved GI database. 4. The input record incorrectly listed 'Noodle' as an ethnic tag — this has been removed as kailan is a vegetable dish, not a noodle dish. 5. Allergens include shellfish (oyster sauce) and soy (soy sauce and oyster sauce); not suitable for shellfish or soy-allergic individuals. 6. Halal status is conditionally true assuming halal-certified oyster sauce is used.
